data.title: Grupo Financiero Banorte: Sit And Wait For The Re-Rate
data.description:
Like many of its Mexican banking peers, Grupo Financiero Banorte (OTCQX:GBOOF) (OTCQX:GBOOY) or Banorte took a beating post-election last year (see Banorte: Mexico's Banking Leader Is Available At A Deep Discount). Since then, the stock, despite trading at historically cheap multiples, has continued to tread water amid political headwinds locally (judicial reforms) and in the US (President Trump’s tariff threats), as well as a front-loaded rate cut cycle by the Mexican central bank.

In recent weeks, however, Mexican equity fortunes have begun to turn, no doubt helped by the Trump administration's decision to push out tariffs for another month. To ride the next leg of the recovery, Banorte stands out as one of the most liquid and higher beta Mexican names, given its leverage to the domestic economy. Also, one of the safest on account of its discounted Price/Book - despite maintaining some very strong loan growth and profitability ratios, as well as one of the most attractive dividend yields in Latin America.

Readthroughs from Q4/2024

Banorte didn’t quite meet the P&L mark, at least on a headline basis, relative to Q4 consensus expectations. A look beneath the hood, however, suggests the group is doing just fine. To recap, performing loans were up +14% YoY, while net interest margins were flat YoY at 6.5%. Banorte Bank margins, on the other hand, were up to a record 6.8%, while margin sensitivity remained low (MXN90m per 100bps rate move) – timely as Mexico enters a rate cut cycle.

On the other hand, operating expenses were up +11% YoY, which kept a lid on earnings growth (+5% for Q4; +7% YoY for the full year). Still, there are caveats – firstly, that the increase was driven by seasonal (and therefore non-recurring) operational expenses and secondly, from reinvestments in tech (Bineo and Tarjeta del Futuro) to the tune of a low-teens % of total revenue. Reported ROE at 21.6% was also down slightly from last year (21.4% in 4Q23), but still quite strong all things considered.

The other notable takeaway from the Q4/FY24 report was Banorte’s higher reported loan-loss provisions and resulting bump up in cost of risk (up ~20bps sequentially to 1.8%). Management did explain on the post-results call, however, that this was really down to state and local government originations (“our government book rose 70% in the year, given the resuming activity following the federal, state and the municipal elections”); these may require upfront provisioning but will “come back in the coming months.” Importantly, management signaled no underlying deterioration in asset quality, so asset quality isn’t a major concern, in my view.

Top-Line Guidance Carries a Touch of Conservatism

Moving onto forward guidance, Banorte’s top-line assumptions appear to be quite conservative. For starters, in line with management’s caution that “'25 is another challenging year. Based upon many issues that are going in Mexico, in the U.S. and in the world,” loan growth is guided to decelerate to +8-11% (vs +14% 2024). Underpinning this are expectations for +0.7-1.3% Mexican GDP growth, “given the effects of the fiscal consolidation, lower inertial momentum and different headwinds from the international environment.”

2025 Guidance

2024

Loan Growth

8% – 11%

14%

GDP

0.7% – 1.3%

1.6%

Source: Banorte

A low bar leaves room for upside. Growth-wise, for instance, if we do see tariffs eventually called off or if trade relations improve with the US, GDP and by extension, loans, should outperform. Management hinted as much, citing “a pickup on that if we see a good evolution on the foreign direct investment that we see the right policy is coming into place.”

More specifically, corporate loans stand out as an area with good leverage to growth swings, potentially “moving into double digit” (vs +9%) in a more favorable macro scenario. The government book will also be one to watch in light of the Q4 pickup and with management citing “aggressive policies concerning the private and public sector working together.”

Bottom-Line Guidance Trickier Amid Interest Rate Cuts

Hitting its profitability targets, on the other hand, may not be as straightforward. For context, Banorte’s earnings growth guidance range for next year stands at +6-11%, with ROE in the 21.5%-23.0% range (vs 22.4% in 2024). Underpinning these are net interest margins of 6.1% to 6.4% (6.4% to 6.6% for the bank), predicated on a Banxico reference rate of 8.5% by year-end.

One issue here is that the central bank’s monetary policy committee has already cut the reference rate by 50bps to 9.50% at its latest meeting; further, forward guidance to “consider adjusting it in a similar magnitude” implies a more aggressive rate cut cycle ahead.

Now, declining rates are good for loan growth, but typically come with a margin compression tradeoff as bank assets reprice ahead of liabilities. Yet, Banorte’s defensive stance, having lowered net interest income sensitivity quite significantly (MXN90m per 100bps change in rates vs a peak of MXN1.2bn per 100bps in Q4 2022), puts it in a great position to defend margins through a front-loaded easing cycle.

The other big bottom-line swing factor is the group’s tech investments; in particular, at its digital bank, Bineo. These investments, a core part of Banorte’s transformation plan, have long-dated payoffs, so will remain a P&L drag for some time. That said, Banorte has also balanced above-par returns on equity with its digital investments for a while now. So, as long as its funding advantages at its core banking business sustains and with NII sensitivity significantly reduced, my base case remains for ‘normalized’ ROEs in the 20% range.

Room for Another Big Dividend/Buyback Year

Banorte’s resilience bodes especially well for the income angle. Recall that 2024 was an exceptionally good year for payouts, with the group distributing almost 90% of its prior year earnings through dividends and buybacks. Yes, this has also cut the CET1 ratio to 13.2% (vs 14.7% in 2023) and with capitalization now in line with its 13.0-13.5% target level (but well above regulatory minimums), the group no longer has an excess capital position.

But as management rightly noted, its balance sheet remains robust enough to not only weather any rate cut headwinds, but also to fund more distributions. To be clear, 2025 likely won’t see as big a payout as 2024, with management committed to a lower 50% regular payout on prior year earnings. Of course, this doesn’t include any special dividends or buybacks; the latter, in particular, is quite likely, given Banorte still has another MXN22bn left in its current buyback authorization. Either way, a ‘normalized’ dividend payout for 2025 alone would still equate to a very attractive ~8% yield - a great return for patient investors willing to sit and wait.

Summing Up

Banorte stock has been de-rated, largely due to external policy headwinds outside of its control. But the clouds are clearing and with the group well-placed to weather an interest rate cut cycle in Mexico, a big re-rating (vs currently discounted levels) could be on the cards. In the meantime, the well-capitalized balance sheet leaves investors with a very attractive ‘income’ proposition, via both the high-single-digit % dividend yield and (likely accretive) buybacks, while investors sit and wait.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
